planting.note: Collect and plant acorns immediately after they fall in autumn; press 1 inch deep in moist, well-drained to moist soil. Darlington oak is native to upland dry sandy soils of the Coastal Plain, distinct from the closely related Q. laurifolia which grows in moist lowlands.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, acidic soil in full sun; Darlington oak is a medium-sized semi-evergreen oak of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain, often confused with the closely related laurel oak (Q. laurifolia) that grows in moister soils. The elliptical leaves are semi-evergreen to evergreen in the deep South, making this an excellent year-round shade and wildlife tree for zone 8–10 landscapes. Like most oaks, it produces abundant small acorns that provide essential food for wild turkeys, wood ducks, blue jays, and white-tailed deer. It is one of the most commonly encountered oaks in dry upland portions of the Florida peninsula and the Gulf Coastal Plain, thriving in the kind of deep, dry, infertile white sand that challenges most trees. An excellent canopy tree for naturalistic landscapes in the coastal Southeast where heat, drought, and sandy soils limit tree choices.

planting.note: Collect and plant acorns immediately after falling in autumn — southern red oak acorns are biennial, meaning they require two growing seasons to mature on the tree. Press acorns 1 inch deep in well-drained, dry, acidic, sandy or loamy soil.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, acidic, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; southern red oak is a dominant canopy tree of the southeastern US Coastal Plain and Piedmont, thriving in the dry, acidic, nutrient-poor sandy soils that characterize much of the region. The distinctive leaves have a broadly lobed base with a narrow middle and a multi-lobed apex — sometimes described as bell-shaped — turning dark red in fall. The tree is a significant mast producer with small, round, bitter acorns consumed by deer, wild turkeys, squirrels, and many other wildlife species. It is more drought-tolerant and better adapted to dry sandy soils than northern red oak (Q. rubra), making it a preferred choice for tough Coastal Plain sites. Historically valuable as a timber tree and important in the ecology of southeastern pine-oak communities.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seeds for 2–3 months at 40°F, then sow in spring; or sow directly in fall for natural stratification. White ash grows quickly in moist, fertile, well-drained soil and is best established from small container plants to avoid disturbing the taproot.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained, deep soil in full sun; white ash is one of the premier native shade trees of eastern North America, valued for its imposing stature, compound leaves that turn purple to burgundy in fall, and quality hardwood used for tool handles, baseball bats, and furniture. The winged samaras (seeds) are eaten by numerous birds including purple finches, pine grosbeaks, and wood ducks; the tree is an important host for numerous native moth and butterfly caterpillars. CRITICAL NOTE: White ash is critically threatened by the emerald ash borer (EAB), an invasive beetle from Asia that has killed hundreds of millions of ash trees across North America since its introduction in Michigan circa 2002. Before planting, consult your local extension service about EAB status in your area and treatment options — prophylactic trunk injection with emamectin benzoate can protect individual trees. Planting in a landscape context where individual trees can be monitored and treated is advisable.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seeds for 30–60 days, or wash seeds from the large fruits and plant in spring; seeds germinate readily in warm soil. For hedgerow use, plant small potted plants 3–4 feet apart in a row and trim annually to form a dense, impenetrable barrier.

directions: Plant in virtually any well-drained soil in full sun — osage orange is extraordinarily tough and tolerates drought, flooding, extreme heat, alkaline soils, heavy clay, and compaction better than almost any other large deciduous tree; it was originally native to a small area of Texas and Oklahoma but was spread across much of North America in the 19th century for use as a living fence (a hedge of osage orange is literally 'horse-high, bull-strong, and hog-tight'). The dense, thorny branches form impenetrable barriers; the orange-colored heartwood is one of the most rot-resistant and hardest of any American wood, traditionally used for fence posts, bows (the tree's French name 'bois d'arc' means 'bow wood'), and railroad ties. The large, lumpy yellow-green fruits (osage oranges or hedge apples) are not edible for humans but are consumed by squirrels and, historically, by mammoths and ground sloths — the tree is a relic of the Pleistocene megafauna era.

planting.note: Scarify seeds or soak in warm water for 24 hours before sowing indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after last frost in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun. Trees can also be started from softwood cuttings in early summer.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; the Japanese raisin tree is an unusual and little-known deciduous tree from East Asia cultivated for its fleshy, branched fruiting stalks (peduncles) that swell and become sweet, raisin-like, and edible in fall when the actual seeds have dropped. It is the peduncles — not the small, round seeds — that are consumed; they have a mild, sweet flavor resembling dried figs or raisins and have been used in Japan, China, and Korea both as a food and as a traditional remedy for alcohol intoxication and liver protection. The tree is an attractive ornamental with large, tropical-looking leaves and fragrant white flowers in summer. In zone 9, it may bloom and fruit erratically due to insufficient winter chilling; it performs best in zones 6–8 where winter cold is reliable. An excellent and unusual fruit tree for the adventurous edible landscape.
